meta.title: Founder Zero → Hero
meta.subtitle: Stage-aware playbook for Indian & US tech founders, grounded in real sources and how top companies did it.
meta.disclaimer: Guidance, not legal/tax/financial advice. Fees, deadlines and program terms change — always confirm on the linked official page before acting. Figures marked 'typical' are market conventions, not rules.

stages.name: Idea & Problem
stages.tagline: Find a real problem worth solving.
stages.focus: Pick a problem you've actually felt, and find the right people to solve it with. Don't incorporate or build much yet.

milestones.text: Pin down one specific problem you've personally felt or seen
Talk to 10+ potential users about the problem (not your solution)
Find a co-founder if you need complementary skills

stages.name: Validate Demand
stages.tagline: Prove people want it before you build a lot.
stages.focus: Get cheap, real signal that people will use and pay. A demo or waitlist beats a finished product nobody asked for.

milestones.text: Ship a cheap signal — landing page, demo video, or waitlist
Get 3–5 people to commit (LOI, pre-order, or design partner)
Define the one metric that proves demand

stages.name: Incorporate & Set Up
stages.tagline: Set up the legal entity the right way for your path.
stages.focus: Pick the entity that matches your funding plans, incorporate, get tax IDs, and set up founder equity with vesting. Getting equity + 83(b) right early avoids painful fixes later.

milestones.text: Choose entity + jurisdiction (India Pvt Ltd vs US Delaware C-corp)
Incorporate and get tax IDs (PAN/TAN or EIN)
Set up founder equity with vesting (file 83(b) within 30 days if US)
Open a company bank account

stages.name: Build the MVP
stages.tagline: Build the smallest thing that delivers the core value.
stages.focus: Ship a minimal product to your first design partners, make your first hire (usually an engineer), and instrument usage so you can see what works.

milestones.text: Ship a minimal version to your first design partners
Make your first key hire (usually an engineer)
Instrument usage so you can see what's working

stages.name: First Customers & Traction
stages.focus: Hand-recruit your first paying customers, find one channel that repeatably brings users, and hit early product/market-fit signals.

milestones.text: Hand-recruit your first 10+ paying customers
Find one channel that repeatably brings users
Hit early PMF signals (retention + early revenue)

stages.name: Raise (Pre-seed / Seed)
stages.tagline: Raise to pour fuel on what's working.
stages.focus: Decide if you should raise at all, pick the right instrument, target the right investors, and consider an accelerator. Raising is a choice, not a requirement.

milestones.text: Decide whether to raise (or stay bootstrapped)
Pick an instrument and target the right investors
Apply to an accelerator if it fits your stage

stages.name: Scale (Series A+)
stages.tagline: Build the machine — Series A and beyond.
stages.focus: Hit the Series A bar (repeatable revenue, clean unit economics), stand up real compliance/finance ops, and build a hiring engine.

milestones.text: Hit the Series A bar (repeatable revenue, clean unit economics)
Stand up real compliance & finance operations
Build a repeatable hiring engine

hiring.summary: Before employees comes the co-founder. Use matching platforms if you don't already have one.

global.where: YC Co-Founder Matching (free, no YC affiliation needed)

incubators.summary: A few programs back you at the idea (even pre-team) stage.

global.note: Talent investor; hubs incl. Bangalore, London, SF. Forms teams from scratch.
US; up to $1M, even pre-idea (equity terms undisclosed).

fundraising.summary: Earliest money is your own, friends/family, angels, or non-dilutive grants. Don't over-raise before signal.

us.action: Raise a first angel check on a SAFE
us.note: Not debt; usually only the valuation cap is negotiated.

india.where: Treelife explainer (CCPS vs SAFE in India)
india.note: India law doesn't recognize a US-style SAFE; iSAFE is structured as CCPS.
Up to ₹20L for PoC/prototype; needs DPIIT recognition, incorporated ≤2 yrs.

incubators.summary: Several top accelerators take idea / pre-product teams.

global.note: $500K on SAFEs; 3-month batch; must relocate to SF for the batch.
Backs pre-product teams; India cohorts run regularly (verify terms per cohort).

hiring.summary: Bring on advisors cheaply with a standard agreement instead of full hires.

global.note: Pre-seed advisors ~0.25–0.5%, ~2-yr vest with a short cliff (typical).

incorporation.summary: Pick the entity that matches your funding plans: Pvt Ltd (India) or Delaware C-corp (US venture path). If you're sure you'll raise from US VCs, many incorporate in Delaware from day one.

india.note: Single form also allots DIN + PAN + TAN and opens a bank account (AGILE-PRO-S). Pvt Ltd is required to raise equity / grant ESOPs.
~₹1,499 + govt fees; bundles PAN/TAN/MOA/AOA/DSC/DIN. (Also Vakilsearch, Cleartax, IndiaFilings.)

us.note: $500 one-time; bundles EIN + 83(b); ~2 business days. Alternatives: Clerky, Firstbase.

us.where: IRS — Apply for an EIN online
us.note: Never pay a fee for an EIN. Foreign founders without SSN file Form SS-4.

us.action: Decide on the India→US flip (or skip it)
us.note: Flip early when valuation is low, or incorporate in Delaware from day one if the US-VC path is certain. Needs Indian + US counsel.

compliance.summary: Get recognized, get your tax registrations, and lock down founder equity correctly from the start.

india.note: Unlocks the 80-IAC tax holiday and other benefits; ~72 hrs.
Mandatory above ₹40L (goods)/₹20L (services), or for any inter-state / e-commerce supply.

us.note: Strict 30-day deadline from stock purchase, no extensions. Confirm with a CPA.
Standard 4-year vest, 1-year cliff; ~95% of VC term sheets require it.

hiring.summary: Your first hire is usually an engineer. Hire from startup-native channels; use contractors to stay lean.

global.action: Learn how to hire your first engineer
global.note: 100% of companies hire ≥1 engineer in their first three hires (YC/Carta-cited).

global.where: Wellfound + YC Work at a Startup
global.note: Equity shown upfront. Also: Work at a Startup (workatastartup.com), HN 'Who is hiring' (hnhiring.com).

global.action: Use vetted contractors to stay lean
global.note: Toptal = screened senior contractors; Upwork = broad/budget. Mind contractor-vs-employee classification.

india.note: Curated, AI-matched startup hiring. Also Hirist (tech), Naukri (breadth), campus placement cells.
Early-stage pool typically 10–15%; first few hires ~1% each; 4-yr vest / 1-yr cliff.

fundraising.summary: If you raise now, it's pre-seed/seed. Know where founders actually raise and what instrument fits your geography.

us.note: Back-a-lead per-deal SPVs. SAFE (post-money) is standard under ~$5M.

india.note: 14,000+ angels/family offices. Also Indian Angel Network and Mumbai Angels.

global.action: Know the seed bar before you raise
global.note: Seed signal ≈ 20+ customers, approaching ~$1M ARR, emerging repeatability.

hiring.summary: As you grow, formalize equity and use an EOR to hire across borders.

global.note: Employer-of-record in many countries; confirm current pricing on each vendor's page.

fundraising.summary: Pick the instrument, understand the ladder, and target investors who write checks at your stage.

us.note: SAFE under ~$5M; priced Series Seed/A above. US median seed raise ≈ $4M (Carta-cited, verify).

india.note: Up to ₹50L for market entry/commercialization. SIDBI FFS funds you indirectly via SEBI-registered AIFs.

incubators.summary: Accelerators give capital + network + a forcing function. Apply where the check and focus match you.

global.note: $500K total on SAFEs ($125K/7% post-money + $375K uncapped MFN).
~$220K ($20K/5% common + $200K SAFE); apply to a specific program.

india.note: Day-zero backing; recent India cohort headline ₹4 Cr (~$470K) for ~11% (verify per cohort).

india.action: Apply to a university / govt incubator
india.note: Strong for India-based deep-tech/early teams. NSRCEL nsrcel.org/programs, SINE sineiitb.org, IIT-M incubation.iitm.ac.in.

fundraising.summary: Series A is a different bar: repeatable revenue and clean unit economics, not just growth.

global.where: ScaleUp Finance — the Series A crunch
global.note: Median Series A now ≈ $2.5M ARR; only ~20% of seed startups reach A (Carta-cited).
After PMF (~$2M ARR): triple x2 years, then double x3.

compliance.summary: Ongoing filings become non-negotiable at scale. Miss them and you risk penalties or disqualification.

india.note: AOC-4 within 30 days of AGM; MGT-7 within 60 days. Late MGT-7: ₹100/day, no cap.
Within 30 days of allotting shares to foreign investors. EPF registration kicks in at 20+ employees.

us.action: Pay Delaware franchise tax the smart way
us.note: Due March 1. Switch to the Assumed Par Value method — the default Authorized Shares method overstates startups' bill by tens of thousands.
A C-corp must file even with $0 revenue. Foreign-qualify in states where you actually operate.

hiring.summary: Turn hiring into a repeatable system.

global.note: Use Greenhouse/Lever/Ashby for pipeline; benchmark equity/cash before scaling offers.

companies.name: Stripe
companies.move: The 'Collison installation' — when someone agreed to try Stripe, the founders said 'give me your laptop' and set them up on the spot.
companies.lesson: Win your first customers by hand — onboard them yourself instead of waiting on a signup link.

companies.name: Airbnb
companies.move: Founders went door-to-door in NYC (YC W09) to recruit hosts and reshoot listing photos.
companies.lesson: When a marketplace won't ignite, serve your earliest users face-to-face before automating.

companies.name: Dropbox
companies.move: Posted a ~3-min demo video to Hacker News before the product was fully built; the waitlist reportedly jumped ~5k → 75k overnight (widely reported).
companies.lesson: A cheap artifact that shows the experience can validate demand faster than code.

companies.name: Figma
companies.move: ~3.5–4 years of stealth R&D (2012 → invite preview 2015 → launch 2016) to make pro design work in a browser.
companies.lesson: When your wedge is a hard technical bet, it can be worth staying heads-down to get the core right.

companies.name: Notion
companies.move: Near-death rewrite (~2015): nearly out of cash, the founders laid off the team, moved to Kyoto, and rebuilt around simplicity.
companies.lesson: If users keep bouncing off, a radical strip-down-and-rebuild can beat patching a flawed base.

companies.name: Postman
companies.move: Started (Bangalore, 2012) as a free Chrome extension scratching the founder's own itch; ~500k users before the company was formally set up.
companies.lesson: A free, zero-friction tool solving your own pain can drive bottom-up adoption that proves demand.

companies.name: Canva
companies.move: Built a narrow precursor first — Fusion Books (2007), a yearbook design tool — and ran it for years before generalizing into Canva (2013).
companies.lesson: Prove your core insight on a narrow, real use case before the big platform bet.

companies.name: Brex
companies.move: Entered YC (W17) to build VR, realized they lacked the expertise, and pivoted to a corporate card for startups — a pain they felt directly.
companies.lesson: Pivot fast toward an acute pain you feel firsthand; proximity to a real problem beats loyalty to the original idea.

companies.name: CRED
companies.move: Launched 2018 as invite-only, gated by a high credit score — a deliberately exclusive, high-trust wedge.
companies.lesson: A deliberately exclusive segment can be a launch strategy — gating concentrates value and makes the product aspirational.

companies.name: Razorpay
companies.move: YC W15; spent the first ~6 months securing bank/regulatory approvals before chasing product.
companies.lesson: In a regulated category, approvals and compliance ARE the foundation — budget months for them before expecting to ship.

companies.name: Zerodha
companies.move: Founded 2010, fully bootstrapped from savings; pioneered flat-fee trades instead of percentage commissions — never needed VC.
companies.lesson: Raising is a choice. A contrarian, cost-led model can be the entire wedge.

companies.name: Flipkart
companies.move: Started (2007) selling only books from an apartment, did everything by hand, and built trust via cash-on-delivery and easy returns.
companies.lesson: Start with one narrow, low-risk category and own the whole experience; removing the customer's risk can matter more than range.

companies.name: Zoho
companies.does: Broad suite of cloud business software.
companies.move: Bootstrapped since 1996 with no VC; built its own talent pipeline via Zoho Schools (training non-degree, rural students).
companies.lesson: You can build a global software company without VC by controlling costs and owning your talent pipeline.
